lesson.learn: Physics is a fundamental science that governs the behavior of energy, matter, and the universe as a whole. However, some extraordinary individuals have managed to push beyond the boundaries set by physics, achieving record-breaking feats in fields like speed, altitude, and strength. These pioneers have inspired awe and sparked curiosity among scientists and the general public alike, challenging our understanding of what is possible. Through their groundbreaking achievements, they have expanded our knowledge of human potential and the capabilities of modern technology. Their innovations have also led to significant advancements in fields such as materials science, aerodynamics, and propulsion systems.
lesson.simpler: Imagine being able to run faster than a speeding bullet or lifting a massive object with ease. That's what some people have achieved by breaking physics records! They've figured out ways to make things go really fast, really high, or really strong â€“ it's like having superpowers!
lesson.keyTakeaway: The most important thing to remember is that these record-breakers are not just pushing the limits of human achievement but also driving innovation and scientific progress. By exploring the boundaries of what's possible, they're helping us better understand the world around us.
lesson.deeperDive: One such example is Felix Baumgartner, who on October 14, 2012, set a new record for the highest altitude jump in history by jumping from over 24 miles above the Earth's surface. This feat was made possible by advanced technology developed by Red Bull Stratos, which included a pressurized suit and a state-of-the-art parachute system. Another notable example is Juliane Koepcke, who survived a plane crash in the Peruvian jungle at the age of 17 and then spent several weeks walking through dense rainforest to safety. Her incredible story sparked a new wave of interest in wilderness survival skills and emergency preparedness. In the realm of speed, Andy Green holds the record for the fastest land speed achieved by a manned vehicle, reaching over 763 mph in his British-built jet-powered car, Thrust SSC. This remarkable achievement was made possible by advances in materials science and propulsion systems developed by Rolls-Royce. The development of these cutting-edge technologies has also led to significant advancements in fields like aerodynamics, allowing for more efficient flight and reducing fuel consumption.
lesson.funFact: You might be surprised to know that some people have even broken records underwater! For example, Ahmed Gabr from Egypt holds the Guinness World Record for the deepest dive ever recorded, reaching an astonishing 332.35 meters (1,090 ft) below sea level in 2014.

people: + Felix Baumgartner - Skydiver and Red Bull Stratos team member
+ Juliane Koepcke - Wilderness survival expert and plane crash survivor
+ Andy Green - Royal Air Force fighter pilot and Thrust SSC driver
+ Ahmed Gabr - Egyptian freediver and Guinness World Record holder
+ Roger Bannister - British medical doctor and first person to run a mile in under 4 minutes
places: + Red Bull Stratos HQ (New York, USA)
+ Thrust SSC Testing Grounds (Sahara Desert, Morocco)
+ The Guinness World Records HQ (London, UK)
years: + 1964 - Roger Bannister becomes the first person to run a mile in under 4 minutes
+ 1930s - Development of the first jet engines
+ 1999 - First human to reach space, Yury Gagarin
+ 2014 - Ahmed Gabr breaks the deepest dive record
concepts: + Wilderness survival skills and emergency preparedness
+ Record-breaking achievements in speed, altitude, and strength
